source.reference: SciBench dataset, source: Engel & Reid 'Thermodynamics, Statistical Thermodynamics, & Kinetics', problem 2.13. Verified by manual calculation: q = I^2 R t = (1.75)^2 * 25.0 * 100 = 7656.25 J; dT = q / (m * Cp) = 7656.25 / (82.5 * 4.18) = 22.2 K; T_final = 300 + 22.2 = 322.2 K.

problem.statement: A system consisting of 82.5 g of liquid water at 300. K is heated using an immersion heater at a constant pressure of 1.00 bar. If a current of 1.75 A passes through the 25.0 ohm resistor for 100. s, what is the final temperature of the water?
problem.task: Calculate the final temperature of the water in K.

mass_water.description: Mass of liquid water in the system

specific_heat_water.description: Specific heat capacity of liquid water at constant pressure

answer.description: Final temperature of the water after electrical heating

answer._comment: Allow ±1 K for rounding in Cp value (4.18 vs 4.184) and intermediate steps

must_include: Calculate electrical energy input using q = I^2 R t or q = V I t
Apply first law at constant pressure to relate heat to temperature change
Use the specific heat capacity of water
must_not_include: Assume the system is adiabatic with respect to the heater
Use Cv instead of Cp for a constant pressure process

domain_context.recommended_thermo_model: First law of thermodynamics at constant pressure (q_p = m Cp dT)
key_assumptions: All electrical energy is transferred to the water as heat (no losses)
Cp of water is approximately constant over the temperature range 300-322 K
Water remains liquid throughout the heating process
common_mistakes: Using the wrong formula for electrical power (P = I^2 R, not P = IR)
Forgetting to multiply power by time to get total energy
Confusing Cp and Cv for a constant-pressure process
Unit errors when converting between J and kJ

reasoning_checkpoints.checkpoint: Calculates electrical energy: q = I^2 * R * t = (1.75)^2 * 25.0 * 100 = 7656 J
Applies the constant-pressure first law: q = m * Cp * dT
Solves for temperature change: dT = q / (m * Cp) ≈ 22 K
Adds dT to initial temperature to get T_final
States that all electrical energy is assumed to transfer as heat to the water

water_remains_liquid._comment: Final temperature must be below boiling point at 1 bar
